content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Just watched "The Lobster" and I'm questioning everything about love

**Score:** 1088
**Body:** My partner and I decided to watch something "different" last night, so we went with The Lobster (2015). I thought it was going to be a quirky rom-com. So I was not prepared. For those who haven't seen it: it's set in a dystopian society where single people have 45 days to find a partner or they get turned into an animal. Also, everyone speaks like an emotionally stunted robot. There’s a scene where a man kicks a child. I REPEAT: A MAN KICKS A CHILD to prove a point to his potential soulmate.

I haven't stopped thinking about it. It's equal parts hilarious, horrifying, and deeply weird. My partner now keeps asking if I'd rather be turned into a lobster or a parrot if things don't work out between us. I am afraid he is hinting at something.

can you reccomend more movies like this? I really need to go on the road started 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Check out the other Yorgos films, but I really really need to recommend Kinds of Kindness, it’s absolutely just weird but so good at the same time. The Favorite is fantastic but the time period may be a turn off. Poor things was also really good and won some academy awards but I personally felt it was in a weird way his big swing for the mainstream.
  - The Killing of a Sacred Deer made me very angry. I’m still angry. But it’s funny. But I’m so, so, so angry.
    - Loved that movie. Turned me on to Barry Keoghan, he’s so good. 

Just watched him in Saltburn and enjoyed that, too!
  - Dogtooth... I'm still thinking about it. I watched it once a few months ago.
    - Dogtooth is my favorite of his.
2. The Killing Of a Sacred Deer.

It's the next logical step after the Lobster. Yargos is a one of a kind filmmaker and his work is going to have that effect on you. It's brilliant.
3. In addition to everything else mentioned, I recommend Todd Solondz movies like Welcome to the Dollhouse and Happiness. Then there’s Noah Baumbach’s The Squid and the Whale.
4. I watched this on 200mg of edibles and it was the most terrifying movie I've ever seen
5. Lars and the Real Girl
6. Wristcutters: a Love Story it’s more rom-com than the Lobster (still not a romcom though) but same level of quirky and also very good

## 2. I want a must watch movie that'll traumatize me

**Score:** 877
**Body:** I watched Requiem For A Dream and it was the worst movie experience of my life. I had a panic attack when the credits started rolling, couldn't sleep at night, and blasted music on my earbuds for the next 10 hours until I passed out from exhaustion only to be greeted by a nightmare of being put in a mental institution. I'm looking for something similar to that. A movie that'll severely fuck me up. But I don't want a bunch of recommendations of Human Centipede or Faces of Death where it's just suffering porn and real life gore for no reason, no, I want a genuinely good movie that fucks you up with intention. Something horrifically gratuitous with a deep message or theme behind it all that I can chew on while the nightmare fuel that I see traumatizes me.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Act of Killing (2012)
2. Watership Down will fuck you the fuck up
3. Deliverance, Blue Velvet, A Clockwork Orange, Last Exit to Brooklyn, and Trainspotting  to an extent were also movies that gave me nightmares for weeks  after seeing them.
If the goal of a film is to make you think of and remember things for a long time afterwards, then they sure did that, but it wasn't really a happy feeling.
4. Irreversible 
  - The first thirty minutes of the film has a background sound with a low frequency of 27 Hz which is almost infrasonic (this sounds like a rumble/vibration), similar types of noises are at times used by police to stop riots. According to Gaspar himself, this could only be heard in theaters (which usually have huge sound systems/subwoofers) and not from headphones or regular home theaters. In humans, this noise causes nausea, sickness, and vertigo. It was one of the reasons people walked out of screenings during the first part of the film. In fact, it was added by Thomas Bangalter (from the electronic music group Daft Punk) to get this reaction.

Couldn't post the IMDb link
5. •Happiness (1998) very chilling ‘comedy’ told across intersecting storyline. Prominent actor: Phillip Seymour Hoffman

•Mysterious Skin (2004) shook me to my core, extremely dark. Prominent actor: Joseph Gordon-Levitt

•Nobody Knows (2004, Japan) sad sad drama that will fuck with you also 

•Kids (1995) NYC, HIV, teenagers. Prominent actor: Chloe Sevigny

•The Florida Project (2017) Poverty and addiction 

all of these have themes of child neglect and the first two have heavy child SA themes so watch with care

Editing to add more as I go
  - Definitely Kids (1995). Came here to suggest it. The ending is burned in my memory.
6. The Deerhunter
7. Precious.
8. Melancholia, In a Glass Cage, The Snowtown Murders, Nothing Bad Can Happen, The Strange Thing About the Johnsons, Our Children (2012), Soft & Quiet, Caché, The Sweet Hereafter, Humanité, Fat Girl, The Vanishing (1988), The Treatment (2014), Mysterious Skin, We Need to Talk About Kevin, The Piano Teacher, Irreversible, Saint Maud, Heavenly Creatures, Breaking the Waves, Bad Lieutenant, A Short Film About Killing, The Seventh Continent, Out of the Blue (1980), The Devil’s Bath, Miss Violence, Tyrannosaur, Revenge: A Love Story, In My Skin, 71 Fragments of a Chronology of Chance, The Free Will, Revanche, White God, Time of the Wolf, In the Company of Men, Birdeater, Under the Sand, The Silence (2010), I’ve Loved You So Long, Seven Days (2010), Un Lac, Proxy (2013), Red White & Blue (2010), The Living and the Dead (2006), Scalene, The Nightingale, First Reformed, The Girl with the Needle

## 3. Looking for a documentary that will leave me shocked and mind-blown 😱✨

**Score:** 853
**Body:** Hey everyone!
I’ve got a week of vacation coming up and I want to make the most of it by watching a documentary that will truly leave me shocked and mind-blown — the kind that makes you rethink everything after watching it. It could be about true crime, science, mysteries, conspiracies, or any topic that’s surprising and impactful.

What are your must-watch recommendations?
Thanks in advance! 🙌



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Up - Series

Made/Directed by Michael Apted

The Up series of documentary films follows the lives of fourteen people in England beginning in 1964, when they were seven years old. The first film was titled Seven Up!

The documentary has had nine episodes—one every seven years—thus spanning 56 years.

Fascinating as you follow the lives of these children well into adulthood.
2. Don’t Fuck with Cats took me a few tries to get past the intro but ended up being a crazy series
3. The Act of Killing

Want to know what happens when mass murdering revolutionaries end up running the country? It gets very, very weird.
4. Grizzly Man
5. Three Identical Strangers
6. Tickled
7. Paradise lost, the documentary about the West Memphis Three. Another one for the “it will piss you off big time” category.
  - I live in Jonesboro where 2 of the 3 were tried. Have you followed the case lately? They are doing DNA testing now on the evidence, even the FAMILIES of the victims wanted it done and the local judge denied it and the DA opposed it also but appeals.court ordered it to be done
8. Girl in the Picture 

Three Identical Strangers 

The Jinx
9.  Blackfish

## 4. Can you recommend a mind-blowing documentary film?

**Score:** 695
**Body:** I’d love to watch a documentary that completely changes the way I see the world. Something intense, eye-opening, or even unsettling. What’s a documentary movie that absolutely blew your mind?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Searching for Sugar Man
2. The Jinx

The Jinx: The Life and Deaths of Robert Durst  
EDIT: adding the full title
3. Three Identical Strangers
  - My cousin directed that. Saw it in the theater.
4. Free Solo
5. Touching The Void
6. My Octopus Teacher
7. Anything by Adam Curtis. They are so good that they stop you in your tracks.
8. The Act of Killing
9. Abducted in Plain Site. Wow. Just wow

## 5. I want movies where nothing explodes... just people being real.

**Score:** 678
**Body:** no spies. no aliens. no twists.
just beautifully written human moments grief, love, awkwardness, growth, healing.
bonus points if it feels like poetry in motion.
got anything like that?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Billy Elliott.
2. The Station Agent, an early Peter Dinklage role

Edit: I'm going to hijack my own comment and also suggest Paddleton (2019) because every time I recommend it here it never gets any kind of response, but it's really fucking good. Ray Romano proves he can act and this movie makes me really hope we get some sort of Romanaissance. Also, would add basically any Duplass brothers production.
3. Lost in translation
4. As Good As it Gets
5. Good Will Hunting
6. Office Space. 

It's full of human moments and mos def poetry in motion :)
7. Harold & Maude
8. The Big Sick
9. No one said Stand by Me?? Pay it Forward, ~~The~~ Scent of a Woman, Searching for Bobby Fischer, Dutch (1991)

~~Edit: as per below correction~~
10. Paris, Texas(1984)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Best comedic moment in a serious scene.

**Score:** 15155
**Body:** This scene in Wolverine was pretty funny.


### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "We're leaving" - Event Horizon
  - "Fuck this ship!"
  - god that shit is too funny

*30 seconds of unspeakable horrifying blood orgy, torture, murder, rape, capped off by dude who ripped his eyes out breaking the fourth wall*

……. “we’re leaving.”

But also, I love how fucking real that is. Fuck that video, fuck this place, fuck you for brining us here, and fuck space in general: we out.
2. “I’m the guy who does his job, you must be the other guy.”  

Either that, or Mr. Wolfe enjoying his coffee.
3. In ConAir, when a guy yells at Cyrus "Cy!" and he responds "onara" and proceeds to set him on fire.
4. "Leave the gun. Take the cannoli"



-The Godfather
5. I also love the time where Prof X and Mags came into the bar and Wolvie told them to "fuck off."
  - Truly one of the best cameos
6. "I know you gentlemen have been through a lot, but when you find the time, I'd rather not spend the rest of this winter TIED TO THIS FUCKING COUCH!" - The Thing (1982)
7. Canadian and American dog tags are very different. I think it was the first Xmen movie that showed wolverine’s tags and they were the Canadian style. That was a nice little touch of detail.

## 2. Movies no one expected to be as good as they were?

**Score:** 14373
**Body:** Don’t get me wrong, I thought Logan was going to be a good movie but I never knew it was going to be a masterpiece. I’m not even a huge fan of the superhero genre but Logan is in a league of its own. Brutal action, great character development and a genuinely compelling story. 

What other movies did no one expect to be as good as they were? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/logan-2017-the-most-violent-superhero-movie

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Emperor's New Groove

Honestly one of Disney's best IMO,. But it went through development hell and hit theaters with very little promotion. Pretty sure it earned it's love through video store rentals. I know I picked it up out of boredom and was then completely blown away.
  - I completely agree! It came out of nowhere and was so original. My family quotes it all the time.
2. The Lego Movie
3. Starship Troopers
4. Big Trouble in little China
5. Galaxyquest. Had very low expectations and was blown away by it.
  - Its hilarious that Tony Shaloub's character was 

A. Faking being east-asian 

B. Blatantly stoned for most of the movie
    - Stoned, but they very carefully do not mention weed or anything else in exposition.  When I first saw it, I guess I missed that part and couldn't understand why he was acting so weird.  When I saw it again later, it made sense, but it's still odd that he's so obviously stoned, but it's never really mentioned.

From what I understand, they took out any mention of it in the editing room to get a PG rating.
6. Goon
  - Man I wish I could see this one for the first time again. I went in completely blind and I was blown away by this one. 

“Two rules, don’t touch my Percocets, and do you have any percocets”

## 3. What movie did you expect to be just good but was actually fantastic?

**Score:** 12908

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pirates of the Caribbean
  
Way back in the olden days of the early ‘00s I thought the idea of a movie based off of a Disney ride was laughable. But I was a fan of Rush, Depp and Bloom (coming off the LOTR train) so I went and saw it. 
  
And shiver me timbers it was frigging spectacular. And it turned into a massive juggernaut that’s made close to $5B.
  - You're saying that the theme park attraction came FIRST and the film came after? What?
    - Yep, someone decided to make a movie about a theme park ride and somehow it worked.
2. Tangled. It didn't sound like my thing, but a friend really wanted to see it and didn't want to go alone. So I went with her, but then I spent the whole movie just totally enraptured by how fun and charming it was. I loved it and I've rewatched it many times by myself since then.
  - Tangled was excellent, it deserves all the hype that Frozen got.
3. Mystery Men
4. Galaxy Quest. Was very funny and the cast was perfect.
  - By Grabthar’s Hammer….What a movie 
5. Shawn of the Dead. I’d heard a little about it and when I had a movie night with some friends a mate gave me shit about it at start and when it finished he apologised and thanked me for picking it.
6. This and bullet train

## 4. What’s the biggest career mistake an actor has ever made?

**Score:** 12145
**Body:** I’ll start with Rod Taylor declining the role of James Bond because he thought the part was “beneath him”

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. McLean Stevenson left MASH after three seasons.  The show went on for 8 more seasons.  Stevenson had very little career success after departing.
2. One day, Arnold Schwarzenegger was bragging to Sylvester Stallone, saying he was gunning for what he thought was the most prestigious role he'd ever do. Really career-changing stuff, the sort of film that creates Hollywood legends. Convinced by the proud boasting of his career competitor and biggest frienemy, Stallone decided to steal the role from Schwarzenegger for himself.

However, Schwarzenegger knew Stallone would do that.  Arnold was actually pranking him into applying for the worst script he'd ever read.

And that's how we got Sylvester Stallone in "*Stop Or My Mom Will Shoot.*"
  - TIL Arnold Schwarzenegger got Sylvester Stallone to take a shitty role in the same way I get my 4 year old to eat a new food
  - Arnold was wild with these head games. If you watch Pumping Iron, you see that he's always pulled shit like that. It's hilarious.
3. Terrence Howard demanding more money from marvel Disney to play war machine
4. Dude that claimed he was beat up by racists because he didn’t think he was getting enough money for the TV show he was doing.
  - You mean Juicy Smooyay?
5. OJ Simpson killing his wife put the kibosh on his acting career
  - At least he can rest in peace now knowing his ex-wife’s killer is dead.
    - I wish Norm MacDonald had lived long enough to deliver that line.

## 5. EuroTrip. Yay or nay?

**Score:** 12015

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yay from me but Scotty doesn't know so don't tell Scotty.
  - I listen to this in the car just driving, unironically one of the most all-out-fun songs ever written.
  - One of the best move tie in songs I’ve ever discovered along with frankly one of the funniest and most unexpected cameos.
    - Matt was in Prague filming The Brothers Grimm and did his cameo as a favour for the writers, who he had known since college.
2. RIP Michelle Trachtenberg…
3. They really are the worst twins ever.
  - “Now that’s some really fucked up shit” 🧚
4. Excellent dumb comedy. This is Bratislava!
5. robot fight 😂
6. Me squzi

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. I rewatched the Shining and realised Jack Torrance literally doesn't work.

**Score:** 2342
**Body:** The job of the Caretaker, as discussed in the Interview in the beginning of the film, is to take care of the Overwatch Hotel. That means doing following a list of taks for maintenance and doing minor repairs to stave off the snow.

But we never actually see doing Jack any of that. Instead we see Wendy going around with a clipboard, checking on the generators and communicating with the outside world.

Jack spends his time bumming around, kicking things over and throwing balls down hallways to never retrieve them.
Wendy takes on his responsibilities as the caretaker in practice, aswell as his responsibilities as a parent. The idea being, of course, that the book Jack is writing will be able to provide for the family.

I read this as a critique of men that see themselves as creatives or even entrepeneurs. Men like Kubrick or King. 
Who think of what they are creating as ultra-important and life changing but really just use that as an excuse to never do any actual work, take on actual responsibilities and do anything useful, instead choosing to disappear into a dreamworld of self-aggrandising creation.

Is that an interpretation you agree with? I haven't read the book. Is this maybe an element there too? 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You're right that Jack is lazy and shuffles the responsibilities of the job off to Wendy, but I don't see why you take this as a critique of King and Kubrick, who are both known for their indefatiguable work ethics. 
  - Right? Saying Kubrick never does any actual work is crazy
    - In OPs defense, Kubrick has been a lazy fuckin slackoff since March of 1999.
      - The man has no moderation. One moment is bouncing off the walls, the next is laying stiff.
        - Hey, it's Kubrick. We saw how many years went between his previous projects. He's probably working on something really special right now and making his actors do thousands of takes until they say and move exactly like he'd want. 

That has to be the only reason it's been so long since his last film, right?
  - Strangely, I think King probably did write The Shining and Jack Torrence's characterization as a kind of self-criticism. It does seem like he does not feel like he works enough or meets his demands as a father -- at least back when he was writing that. Hopefully, he now thinks that it all worked out fine in the end. He's still married (against all odds, really) and his children are doing okay.
    - I think there is a lot of King in the character. Torrence is all about living with the fear of an addiction that takes over and destroys everything. This gets emphasized a little bit more in the book, but it's a theme of the movie too. A lot of King's characters express similar fears.
    - He has publicly stated this before.  He was writing about a drunk who destroyed his family at a time in his life when he was a drunk who's behavior was doing the same.  The book was his internal coping as he came to terms with himself.

He famously hates the movie because he feels like it veered away from that and leaned more into the ghost aspect.
2. I think reading it as a critique of "Men like Kubrick or King", who are/were both notoriously hard-working in their respective fields, seems like a massive stretch. 

King may well be engaging in a degree of self-critique when he makes his protagonists writers (which he often does), but I don't think you've done much to extrapolate that strain from the movie - if it's even there. 

Kubrick has never struck me as a particularly introspective director.
3. There is more actual work in the beginning of book iirc and his decline in work is part of his overall decline / struggle. The movie obviously had to be more succinct, but i still think this is a sorta shared theme. Jack is struggling with who he thinks he "is" who he thinks others see him as and the person he wants to be, and work is central to that. I think its true across genders, but particularly in men, who have been seen as "bread winners" - work is a core part of identity.

## 2. Is Hollywood really dying?

**Score:** 648
**Body:** I keep seeing people say “hollywood is dying” and honestly I’m starting to wonder if it’s true because I myself never watch movies nowadays I mostly just go through tiktok or play grizzly's quest. It feels like every other week there’s news about box office bombs like endless reboots and sequels nobody asked for and audiences just not showing up like they used to. Streaming has completely changed how people watch movies and a lot of the hype that used to surround big releases just isn’t there anymore. On top of that so many people complain that hollywood has lost its originality. It’s either superhero movies, remakes or giant CGI spectacles that all feel the same. Meanwhile a lot of smaller and more creative projects are either getting buried or ending up on streaming platforms where they don’t get the same attention.

But then again hollywood has been “dying” in people’s eyes for decades and it always seems to adapt somehow. So is this just another phase or are we actually watching the decline of the hollywood machine as we knew it?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I work(ed) in the entertainment industry in LA. Yes, it is. Most of us are out of work when we were working very steadily before. A lot of production is moving out of LA. Staff on tv shows is being cut to an unsustainably small amount of people. Anyone who doesn't live in LA and doesn't have any ties to the entertainment industry doesn't know what they're talking about. Unless you experience it or hear countless tearful stories from your friends, you wouldn't really know what's going on right now.
  - When would you say this trend started?
2. Depends what 'dying' is defined as?

In general Hollywood still has a lot of power but it's on the wane, the rise of Asia and tax incentives outside of the USA has made a real impact. We see Asia being a real powerhouse with Manga/Anime from Japan, TV/Films from Korea and China starting to see a real rise in the domestic market for China made films/media.

You also see a massive cut in work in America, Netflix is pushing more money over to Asia for new productions instead of America. The audience want Asian media & it costs less to make, it's win win for the money men.

Still at the same time Hollywood has a lot of power/money, it's going to be a long time before it's lost relevance.
  - That’s part of the reason. But as somebody based in Asia, things aren’t looking so rosy here either. 

Cinema attendance in Korea is significantly down, to the point that it’s a major concern for the Ministry of Culture. 

Meanwhile in China, the indie film market has been completely decimated by policy changes. Many seasoned indie producers aren’t able to get any new projects off the ground and are switching industries. 

Japanese cinema hasn’t been on great health for a while.

In general, television is still doing well, and of course Korean TV in particular is making a big impact globally; but cinema across Asia is struggling. 
  - A consequence of streaming further operating as "global" companies. Hollywood has long tried to appeal to international markets, but the streaming model & companies that own them have essentially delivered it to its logical end: the U.S is simply a smaller audience than many other parts of the world. The companies here are making more shows for other countries and less for our local audience.
    - >Hollywood has long tried to appeal to international markets

Businessmen with no real interest in film becoming execs and producers, along with capitalism in general, has definitely been a huge part of the decline.
  - If you go look at the stuff on Netflix right now, SO MUCH of it is done overseas.

I mean, it used to be 5% of Netflix.  It's got to be closer to 25% now.
3. Hollywood isn’t dying. It’s bleeding out under corporate control and cultural change.

Studios have been absorbed by corporations that care only about quarterly returns. That shift siphoned off the artistry. Not gone entirely, but the default mode is product, not film. The aim is to capture dollars, not move people.

Culture shifted too. Theaters used to be the place. Now they’re a luxury. A ticket, popcorn, and soda can run fifty bucks. That’s not inflation alone. Those numbers are baked in by deals between studios and theater chains. On top of that, people are trained to expect the pause button and home setups that look sharper than what a lot of theaters can deliver.

And then there’s the theater experience itself. Phones glowing, people talking, constant distractions. Immersion dies in that setting.

As for superhero movies, they’re not the core issue. Every era had its wave: musicals, westerns, gangster flicks. The real problem is volume without care. When the market floods itself with interchangeable product, audiences check out.

So no, Hollywood isn’t dying. It’s just stuck in a loop of self-cannibalization, feeding shareholders while training audiences to stay home. The machine still hums , but its pulse is weak.
  - Man, I've been noticing that too. The last few movies I went to, some people were just talking over the movie like it was background noise and other people were yelling at them.

## 3. On Ridley Scott's no-****s-given phase. Who are his recent films really for?

**Score:** 606
**Body:** I watched Gladiator 2 at the weekend, and my over-riding impression was that Ridley Scott is now deep into his no-fucks-given phase. The movie is ugly to look at, with weird and artificial lighting, coupled with obvious CGI. The plot is overly-convoluted and the action sequences are more ridiculous than exciting. It’s far worse in every respect than Gladiator or Kingdom of Heaven, from over 20 years ago. However it’s not a disaster. The acting is great across the board, with Paul Mescal being particularly good. And it’s never boring exactly. 

Making something like Gladiator 2 is beyond most film-makers. But it’s hard to look at the opening scene with CGI ships with siege towers, under a strangely coloured sky, and see anything good in it. And yet he presumably signed off on that as being what he wanted. 

This has been going on for years now. Prometheus looked gorgeous and had some great scenes, but really half-assed it when it came to the plot and the characters. Napoleon somehow turned one of the most exciting lives in history into a tale of a middle-aged bore. But although they’re deeplyflawed, I still get the impression that these ARE the films that Scott wanted to make. They didn’t fail because of lack of skill or experience, as he clearly has those. They failed because of strange artistic choices.

So what do you think is going on in the mind of the director? What’s he trying to achieve, and who are his recent films actually for? He obviously knows what he’s doing and how to make great films. But why is he now seemingly only interested in spectacle, when his greatest films had spectacle AND good stories? 

You can’t really argue that he’s only interested in the money, as his films are far from being all box office successes. But nor does he seem to be pursuing some kind of personal artistic vision. Could it be that he’s only making films because it’s his job, and studios are happy to give him the work? 

All opinions welcome!





### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's always been like, we've just sorta forgotten about a lot of the messy misses early in his career or they've been reclaimed. Kingdom of Heaven and even Blade Runner were only reclaimed after post-release edits. Hannibal is the film after the original Gladiator and feels just as much like a "who is this for?" as Gladiator II.
  - That is true. Of his 30 films, I’d say about 10 are fantastic, 10 are awful and 10 are acceptable/forgettable. It’s just that the fantastic ones are SO good and so prominent in the public attention that it’s easy to overlook the bad stuff.
    - It wouldn't have mattered if he directed 50 House of Guccis in his career, because he directed Alien and Blade Runner. And to some people, that's all you need
      - You need thelma and Louise as well
2. I don't know but these are my two cents it kind of comes across as the same as Clint Eastwoods sort of vibe. They're just two old dudes who like making movies and have done it most of their lives and probably don't know what to do with them selves if they stopped so they just keep on going. 

I mean just look at Aaron Rodgers for example. He has all the money in the world with little to prove but he just can't hang it up quite yet because he's loves his job. I think it's that simple to be honest. 

The movies are for him and not to watch but actually something for him to do. Can't say I blame him
3. I think he just likes the work. Even in his prime, I don't think Ridley Scott ever had any recognisable style, with very little in his films really evocing his specific voice. I think a lot of his greatest films are a result of great collaboration between himself and writers, editors, actors, set decorators, cinematographers, etc. The Last Duel, a film that was released during his "no shits given" phase, was pretty fantastic, and I believe it's because it was a great script and a lot of elements came together for it. Films like Nepoleon and Gladiator 2 had pretty poor scripts in comparison.
  - Ridley lives and dies on the quality of his scripts. I don’t even know if he can tell a good script from a bad one, but his movies sure know the difference.
    - Exactly this. I know people who have worked with him, and they assure me that he does not give a flying fuck about the quality of the script – he just wants to shoot a movie.
  - Agreed completely. Ridley has undeniable talent, but he's a very collaborative director. He's not an auteur. Alien is what it is because of the script and, most importantly, HR Giger's Xenomorph design and Stan Winston's special effects work. Ridley was obviously instrumental to that movie, but without those other people, Alien might have been a dud instead of a classic.
    - Don't exclude Dan O'Bannon. In many ways, Alien is the result of the teambuilding work done by Jodorosky for his failed version of Dune.

## 4. Why does Hollywood always show poverty as dirty?

**Score:** 407
**Body:**  In movies, poverty is almost always shown as “dirty.” Messy homes, grimy clothes, run-down neighborhoods.
Sure, poverty can mean tough living conditions. But it doesn’t always mean filth. Many people struggling financially still keep their spaces clean and full of dignity.

It makes me wonder, are these portrayals shaping how we see class and reinforcing old stereotypes? Or do they reflect reality in some places?

Seems to me like most movies have a lazy bias. I grew up pretty poor and most of my neighbors, family and friends too. Our homes were not messy, dirty, maybe some clutter but not dirty or grimy. Thoughts?

4. Having lived in actual poverty, it is dirty. You may keep your space clean, but the environment is less well maintained and cared for than in affluent communities. 

Major metropolitan areas in particular are especially grimy. You could be the most cleanly individual. Wont change a thing if your apartment neighbors have rat and roach infestations due to negligence

More specifically, a lot of cinematic language that is still used to describe poverty was popularized in the 70s and 80s, when American metropolitan areas in particular where a completely unrecognizable type of dirt and grime. ## 5. Vague dissatisfaction with Weapons movie

**Score:** 352
**Body:** Certain movies nowadays like Talk to Me, Hereditary, It Follows, the Babadook, and the Witch could be called [art horror or elevated horror](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Art_horror) in part because they serve as a vehicle for underlying messages. They're like cautionary tales, holding a mirror to society and opening our imaginations to question our humanity more deeply and step into new perspectives. Their intentional motifs, symbols, changing character motivations, and thematic explorations all inspire curiosity that we can take home to help us understand real-world issues.

Weapons is a hit with a great box office performance and high scores from critics and audiences. While I enjoyed it, based on the trailer, marketing, title, and first five minutes, I'm guessing I may not be alone in expecting it to have presented a meaningful message of some kind, for example, about what leads to a tragic event and how a community processes trauma around it. While it did a great job maintaining the momentum of its tricky, mystery-driven plot, I left the theater feeling like it didn't fully cash the checks it wrote. 

It calls to mind real-world tragedies like school shootings, for example, when >!a character briefly dreams about a gun floating above a house!<. It's a moment that stands out, but in retrospect feels more hand-wavy than meaningful. The tone is different, like we've been teleported to Twin Peaks for just those few seconds. There may be purpose behind it, but the writer/director seems to have shrugged it off in interviews. 

Also detracting from a cohesive message, I feel like the movie takes seemingly unnecessary detours--a sequence of minor incidental mysteries, such as >!the vandalized vehicle and the attack at the gas station!<. While the interplay of all the focus characters keeps things fresh, several plot lines such as those of >!the cop and addict!< just feel like vehicles for plot reveals. They don't tie directly or metaphorically to critically unpackable subject matter. The characters might even be called flat, as they don't evolve in their decisions or beliefs but are instead whipped around by circumstance.

I feel like there are so many thematic complexities that a movie about the disappearance of children could explore. And while Weapons sets the table well at the start to tap this potential, by the time the credits roll, themes seem more like afterthoughts tacked on, rather than core themes tackled head on. If the intent is to explore the ripple effects of collective trauma, such as grief causing community members in the wake of a tragedy to turn on each other, I couldn’t follow that thread either. And after >!the antagonist is defeated, I’m left wondering “so what?” We had only just learned she exists, and some of her feature scenes flip the tone of the movie in directions I’d consider interesting but unnecessary.!< 

I think the unresolved feeling I get from the movie is because while it has the air of having something to say, the act of sussing out what exactly feels murky. If you felt like it did hit the mark in this way, I'm interested to hear about it.

1. Not sure why you’re getting so much hate with your post, thought it was a great jumping off point for discussion.

For me, the excellence of the film comes from the bringing together of small vignettes in an interesting storytelling style very well executed. In addition to fabulous performances, compelling camera work, and a fresh take on a fairly simple witchcraft/dark magic/dark forces bound antagonist. 

Your critique is valid to your taste, I just think the reason it is being so heralded are what I like to think of as the “bones” or the underlying movie making techniques that make a movie entertaining and enjoyable beyond the presence of a motif or theme woven into each moment.
  - Absolutely. I found Weapons to be refreshingly well crafted and mysterious. Nothing was overworked, nothing was underworked and a cathartically beautiful unhappy ending that didn’t fall into the typical trappings of North American horror.
2. I enjoyed it as an exercise in style and mood over theme. In an age where trauma porn has been so over done as to become positively tropey, pure visceral style and mood setting is a perfect antidote to what has become an overly “serious” and somber genre at the “high end”.  I view it almost as a dialect response to Bring Her Back, which for me set the high water mark for trauma misery as attempted high art. 

From what I understand, the film is intended to mirror a dream in many ways and imagery is not intended to always be completely literal or sensible. I love that there are people willing to put that to screen, without trying to satisfy the chattering intellectual class who want films to be puzzles they solve, as opposed to art they experience.
  - I’m not quite sure how this is any different than Hereditary, Longlegs and Us - which all play with mixing tones, camp/absurdity and pure mood. These are not remotely serious films. I think the somber, self-serious horror films are almost in the minority now. 

I don’t see it as very dreamlike either (especially anything like lynch) aside from two clearly delineated dream sequences - and thought it was a pretty straight forward and didactic.

  - The notion of "elevated horror" feels reminiscent to me of the idea of "revisionist Westerns," where you watch these movies like McCabe and Mrs Miller and you're like wow, this is so dark, it's subverting these myths of the Old West, and then you go back and watch like... Fort Apache, and you're like oh wait, this is also so dark, and this is also subverting these myths of the Old West lol
    - In the case of elevated horror, it's people who know nothing of "genre" being embarrassed about liking "genre" and twisting themselves into pretzels trying to say that they're favorite "genre" stuff is actually very special absolute cinema.

Horror has always been primarily about telling a good story in the sense that the story would create an emotional and aesthetic effect on the reader. It's not fundamentally about the message, although it will have themes relevant to the world no matter what. But it's fine when there's a message too, which is anyway the case for a ton of horror works which the average plebeian who thinks he's a patrician would dismiss as "genre". More and more people seem to think nowadays that the epitome of smart and meaningful horror is to pull a "all in le head lmao (the monster was trauma all along!)", which is really funny.

Revisionist westerns are a bit different in that they don't need to be dark, nor are they assumed to be "better" than a very regular western. Examples of nonconformist westerns are pretty old as you said, but at some point going against the grain became very prevalent. With elevated horror it's really nothing more than ignorant people trying to make sense of the fact that contrary to what they had always believed, horror isn't just about some guy jumping at the screen or crass stuff. It's not subverting or criticizing anything about horror as a "genre".
  - I think some viewers, for one reason or another, think the horror genre is somehow beneath consideration, and have to attach an additional descriptor to make it sound more worthwhile.
5. >While the interplay of all the focus characters keeps things fresh, several plot lines such as those >!of the cop and addict!< just feel like vehicles for plot reveals. 

Isn't this kind of the point? The plot is driven by a series of vignettes, each one primarily focused on a character. Each one ties into something odd that happens at the end of the previous one. They reveal pieces of the story. I found it intriguing. If it didn't work for you, that is understandable. I guess I find it a bit reductive to say that *some* of them were vehicles for plot reveals -- they all were exactly that. 

Not every character sequence has a direct tie-in to the overall mystery, but I don't see any issue with that. They are characters, and instead them being lifeless plot devices, they have dramatic things going on in their lives. It's nothing too deep, but it gives them a bit of humanity. I didn't time them, but I don't feel like any were overly long.
